public_question: Is the first liquid out of an espresso puck weak because water only just entered, or can it already be near-saturated?
headline: The first liquid out of this espresso puck was already about 97% as concentrated as the peak.
plain_language_finding: In one pressure-controlled, fraction-resolved dataset, the earliest measured fraction was already ~97% of the peak TDS. This separates WETTING time (water crossing the puck) from DISSOLUTION speed (coffee going into solution) — the liquid can take time to appear while becoming chemically concentrated almost immediately.
uncertainty_or_sensitivity: The first fraction has only ONE replicate; the 23 s boulder-diffusion timescale is the comparator that near-instant dissolution beats — favouring rapid dissolution in this configuration.
validity_range: One rig, one coffee, one configuration (pressure-controlled).
primary_caveat: One rig/config and a single first-fraction replicate — a strong prompt for replication, NOT a universal law. Do not claim all espresso reaches full strength instantly.
practical_implication: Collect TIMED FRACTIONS rather than judging extraction from the final cup; do not assume the first drops are chemically weak.
reproduction: python -c "from puckworks.harness import dissolution_speed_test as f; print(f())"

public_question: When a flow curve dips and recovers, does that prove the coffee bed changed (swelling, channeling, fines)?
headline: A flow dip can come from the espresso machine even when the puck model does nothing — but some traces DO need a time-varying bed.
plain_language_finding: A machine-only model (pump + headspace, no bed mechanism) can reproduce a dip-and-recovery, so that shape alone is not a diagnosis. Separately, on a 9-bar rising-flow trace, every constant baseline fails and a time-varying porosity trajectory reconstructs it ~4.9x better — time variation IS needed there (though a flexible non-mechanistic curve does as well, so this does not identify a specific bed mechanism).
uncertainty_or_sensitivity: Single 9-bar trace, one window (15-95 s); a 4-parameter flexible cubic reaches ~0.096 g/s, so the RMSE gap shows time-variation is needed, not that a particular mechanism is identified.
validity_range: One rig; the machine-null is on Foster's fitted trace, the ladder on the Waszkiewicz 9-bar rising-flow window.
primary_caveat: Sufficient, NOT unique: do not say 'dissolution caused the rising flow'. A flow curve becomes informative only with more observables and controlled perturbations.
practical_implication: Do not diagnose swelling/channeling from a dip-and-recovery alone; log pressure, flow, first-drip time, mass, control mode, and ideally timed TDS fractions.
reproduction: python -c "from puckworks.harness import kappa_t_ladder as f; print(f())"

public_question: How can an exciting result weaken when the target variable and its uncertainty are audited?
headline: We thought a model had explained the fine-grind espresso dip — then we checked the units and the raw replicates.
plain_language_finding: After rebuilding the target as a coherent TDS-derived extraction yield (an earlier version mixed mg solute masses with g TDS), the raw measured cells are ORDERED across dial (18.27 -> 19.38 -> 19.62%), with the middle dial BELOW the coarse one — no interior 'fine-grind' maximum. A channeling model can still MAKE such a bump, but that is model capacity, not identification: the bump is fragile and smaller than the shot-to-shot spread.
uncertainty_or_sensitivity: Welch 95% CI on the middle-vs-coarse contrast excludes zero; the model's interior bump is near-flat at 9 bar and appears in only a minority of the closure grid — smaller than replicate spread. Means are described as ORDERED, not 'statistically monotone' (the 1.4-vs-1.7 interval includes zero).
validity_range: Schmieder E65S grinder DoE, fixed central flow/temperature condition; TDS-derived EY observable.
primary_caveat: These are Schmieder E65S grinder-DIAL positions — a dial number is NOT a particle size and is non-portable to another grinder without a calibrated adapter. Do not read a universal 'too-fine optimum'. Also: static channeling remains a viable GENERATOR (model capacity), the data just do not identify it as the cause.
practical_implication: Plot raw replicates before trend lines, use a coherent yield measure, and distinguish a machine-specific dial position from a physical grind characteristic.
reproduction: python -c "from puckworks.harness import result1_magnitude_comparison as f; print(f())"

public_question: Does combining two plausible mechanisms automatically make a more realistic espresso model?
headline: Adding a swelling branch to the extraction model made the prediction worse than a flat line.
plain_language_finding: In a shared-porosity composition, extraction-only reduces exactly to the poroelastic rung; adding an imported swelling branch OVER-closes the shared porosity, so the combined flow prediction FLATTENS and scores WORSE than a best-fit constant baseline. The lesson is not that swelling is absent — it is that two individually plausible branches can be incompatible when they share a state variable.
uncertainty_or_sensitivity: Over the same 15-95 s window, the composite 0.648 g/s is worse than the best-constant null (~0.57 g/s, see PV-02) and the extraction-only rung (~0.12 g/s) — a diagnosed mis-scale, reported not tuned away.
validity_range: 9-bar Waszkiewicz trace; the imported swelling parameters are mo2023_2's, applied to a saturated pre-wet rig.
primary_caveat: Diagnoses THIS shared-porosity composition, not the existence of swelling; and 'simple is always best' is NOT the lesson — complexity is justified when it beats the baseline.
practical_implication: Do not assume a more elaborate model is more trustworthy; a simple baseline must stay visible and be beaten.
reproduction: python -c "from puckworks.models.brewer2026.coupled_kappa_t import composition_residual as f; print(f())"

public_question: Can a model match the final cup while leaving its hidden rate and inventory unresolved?
headline: The final cup can hide very different extraction clocks.
plain_language_finding: Across the tested endpoint data, many inventory-rate combinations fit nearly equally well (log-parameter SSE-Hessian condition number ~1,930; the 10%-near-optimal rate set covers ~76% of the tested grid). Held-out cup predictions remain reasonably stable, but the mechanistic model (8.2% MAPE) barely improves on a level-only constant (8.6%) and is worse on 50 of 108 held-out points. Timed fractions preserve rate information the final cup discards.
uncertainty_or_sensitivity: Practical (not structural) non-identifiability; no likelihood/noise model. The tested rate range is RIGHT-CENSORED at its upper edge. The inverse-curvature coupling is objective-surface geometry, NOT a statistical parameter correlation; the 10% set is a tested-domain threshold set, NOT a confidence interval.
validity_range: One model refit to one independent campaign; conditional on the tested model, observation operator, data, objective, and matched-endpoint domain.
primary_caveat: Endpoint prediction stability is NOT parameter identification and NOT mechanistic validation; the mechanistic model barely beats a level-only null. Not a claim of structural identifiability, and not evidence the mechanism transferred.
practical_implication: To learn mechanism, preserve the clock (collect timed fractions), measure the soluble inventory independently, and beat a simple level-only null before crediting a mechanism from endpoint accuracy.
